question: [File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getBalancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user observe that getBalancesQuery() embeds the tokenBalanceSubQuery as a correlated subquery for every row returned, and by requesting a large limit with a timestamp filter cause the server to execute N correlated subqueries against the token_balance table, creating a resource exhaustion condition that delays legitimate reward collection transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getOptimizedTimestampRange()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a timestamp=lt:<value> operator where value is exactly 1 nanosecond, causing getOptimizedTimestampRange() to compute ltValue = value - 1n = 0n as the upperBound, and since lowerBound (set to first day of previous month) > 0n, the function returns an empty object — silently suppressing the balance query — which could be used to probe the system's behavior around the epoch boundary and identify edge cases in reward accounting?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getBalances()] Can an unprivileged external user combine account.id=<specific_account> with multiple timestamp=ne:<ts> operators to exclude all balance snapshots except one specific historical snapshot for a target account, obtaining the exact balance at a chosen point in time to reconstruct the account's reward accumulation history and determine the optimal time to trigger a theft?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountBalanceTimestampRange()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a timestamp range that spans exactly the staking period boundary (midnight UTC) to getAccountBalanceTimestampRange(), causing the function to return a snapshot timestamp that is the last snapshot before the reward calculation, and use the returned balance data to determine which accounts had the highest stake_total_start and thus the largest pending_reward at that moment?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getBalances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the /api/v1/balances endpoint does not include pending_reward in its response (unlike /api/v1/accounts), and by comparing the balance from /api/v1/balances with the balance from /api/v1/accounts for the same account, compute the pending_reward value for any account without needing to query /api/v1/accounts directly, enabling mass enumeration of pending rewards across all accounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: parseAccountIdQueryParam()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an AccountAlias in the account.id parameter to parseAccountIdQueryParam(), which calls EntityService.getAccountIdFromAlias() asynchronously, and by observing response timing differences determine whether a specific alias-addressed account exists and has a balance above the staking threshold, enabling targeted reward theft reconnaissance without knowing the numeric account ID?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getOptimizedTimestampRange()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a timestamp=gte:<future_date> operator with a date far in the future to getOptimizedTimestampRange(), causing effectiveUpperBound = bigIntMin(upperBound, nowInNs) = nowInNs, and lowerBound = bigIntMax(gte_value, optimalLowerBound) = gte_value which is greater than nowInNs, resulting in lowerBound > upperBound and an empty return — but the attacker can use binary search on the gte value to determine the exact current time in nanoseconds, enabling precise timing attacks against reward distribution?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getTokenBalanceSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user observe that getTokenBalanceSubQuery() limits token balances to tokenBalanceLimit.multipleAccounts per account, and by requesting balances for accounts with exactly that many tokens, determine which accounts are at the token balance limit — a characteristic of high-value accounts that are also likely to have large pending staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getBalances()] Can an unprivileged external user issue a GET /api/v1/balances?timestamp=<ts>&account.balance=gte:10000000000 request to identify all accounts with balances above 100 HBAR at a specific historical snapshot, then cross-reference with the current /api/v1/accounts endpoint to find accounts where balance has not changed (no transactions) but pending_reward has accumulated, identifying the highest-value uncollected reward targets?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountBalanceTimestampRange()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountBalanceTimestampRange() uses `order by consensus_timestamp desc limit 1` to always return the most recent snapshot within the range, and by supplying a timestamp range that ends just before a reward payout snapshot, force the API to return the last pre-payout balance — which is higher than the post-payout balance for accounts that donated rewards — creating a false impression of available funds?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getBalancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user observe that getBalancesQuery() constructs the WHERE clause by joining [tsQueryResult.query, accountQuery, accountIdsQuery, balanceQuery] with ' and ', and that accountIdsQuery is built from unparameterized integer IDs in getAccountIdsByPublicKey(), and craft a public key that returns account IDs containing SQL metacharacters (e.g., via integer overflow or unexpected ID format) to corrupt the WHERE clause and bypass balance filters that protect reward-eligible account enumeration?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getBalances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the order parameter (asc/desc) in getBalances() to control the pagination anchor in formatBalancesResult(), which uses the last element of ret.balances as anchorAccountId, and by alternating between asc and desc orders reconstruct the complete sorted list of accounts with their balances, enabling efficient identification of the highest-balance accounts with the largest pending staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getOptimizedTimestampRange()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a combination of gt: and lt: operators with values that differ by exactly 1 nanosecond to getOptimizedTimestampRange(), causing lowerBound = gt_value + 1n = lt_value - 1n + 1n = lt_value, and since lowerBound equals upperBound (lt_value - 1n), the range is valid but covers only a single nanosecond — allowing the attacker to probe for balance snapshots at exact nanosecond timestamps and reconstruct the precise timing of reward distributions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountIdsByPublicKey()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a public key that matches the staking reward account (0.0.800) or treasury account (0.0.2) to getAccountIdsByPublicKey(), causing those system accounts to be included in the `ab.account_id in (${ids})` filter, and retrieve the balance of the staking reward pool account to determine how much is available for pending reward payouts — enabling the attacker to time their theft attempt when the pool is at maximum?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getBalances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getBalances() with no timestamp filter reads from the entity table using `balance_timestamp as consensus_timestamp`, and that balance_timestamp may be significantly older than the current time for accounts with no recent transactions, causing the API to return stale balances that do not reflect recent reward payouts — allowing the attacker to identify accounts where the entity balance is stale and the pending_reward has been accumulating undetected?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: formatBalancesResult()] Can an unprivileged external user observe that formatBalancesResult() uses `rows.map((r) => r.consensus_timestamp).reduce((result, current) => (result > current ? result : current), 0)` to compute the response timestamp, and that this timestamp is the maximum across all returned accounts — not the actual snapshot timestamp — causing the reported timestamp to be misleading when accounts have different balance_timestamp values, enabling the attacker to misinterpret the freshness of reward data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getBalances()] Can an unprivileged external user issue a GET /api/v1/balances request with account.id=<staking_reward_account_0.0.800> to retrieve the balance of the staking reward pool account, and by monitoring this balance over time determine when large reward distributions are about to occur (balance decreases), enabling the attacker to time social engineering or on-chain attacks against reward recipients?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getOptimizedTimestampRange()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a timestamp=ne:<ts> operator for every known balance snapshot timestamp within a month to getOptimizedTimestampRange(), causing the neParams array to grow large and the `not consensus_timestamp = any ($4)` condition in getAccountBalanceTimestampRange() to exclude all snapshots, returning an empty result and causing getTsQuery() to return no query — effectively making the balance endpoint return empty results and denying service to legitimate reward monitoring?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getTokenAccountBalanceSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user observe that getTokenAccountBalanceSubQuery() reads from token_account where `associated is true` and orders by token_id, and by requesting balances for a specific account determine which tokens that account holds — information that, combined with the HBAR balance, reveals the full asset profile of a staking account including the value of assets that would be lost if the account's pending rewards were stolen?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getBalancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getBalancesQuery() uses `?::bigint as consensus_timestamp` (the first tsParams element, which is `upper`) as the reported timestamp for all rows, regardless of the actual consensus_timestamp of each account's balance row, causing the API to report a uniform timestamp that may not reflect when each individual account's balance was last updated — misleading reward monitoring systems about the freshness of balance data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getBalances()] Can an unprivileged external user combine account.publickey=<key> with account.balance=gte:<threshold> in getBalances() to find all accounts sharing a specific public key that also have balances above the staking threshold, identifying multi-account staking setups where a single key controls multiple accounts with large pending rewards — enabling a single targeted attack to steal rewards from multiple accounts simultaneously?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountBalanceTimestampRange()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a timestamp range that spans multiple monthly partitions by setting lowerBound to a date two months ago and upperBound to now, causing getAccountBalanceTimestampRange() to search across multiple time partitions for the treasury account's balance snapshot, and use the returned snapshot timestamp to determine the exact cadence of balance snapshot generation — information useful for timing attacks against reward collection?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getBalances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the entity_balance CTE in the no-timestamp path reads `balance_timestamp as consensus_timestamp` from the entity table, and that accounts with no recent transactions may have very old balance_timestamps, to identify dormant high-balance accounts that have been accumulating staking rewards for extended periods without collecting them — making them high-value targets for theft?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: parseAccountIdQueryParam()] Can an unprivileged external user supply multiple account.id= parameters with different operators (e.g., account.id=gte:X&account.id=lte:Y) to parseAccountIdQueryParam() in balances.js, which uses a different implementation than utils.parseAccountIdQueryParam(), and exploit any behavioral difference between the two implementations to bypass account ID range validation and retrieve balances for accounts outside the intended range — including system accounts with reward-related balances?

[File: 'File Name: rest/balances.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getOptimizedTimestampRange()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a timestamp=lte:<ts> where ts is exactly at the boundary between two monthly partitions to getOptimizedTimestampRange(), causing optimalLowerBound = getFirstDayOfMonth(effectiveUpperBound, -1) to be set to the first day of the month two months ago, expanding the search window to cover three monthly partitions instead of two, and potentially returning balance snapshots from a wider range of staking periods?
